module.title: Piano Roll Mastery
module.description: Go beyond placing random notes — learn to draw melodies with purpose, stack chords that actually sound good, use ghost notes for context, add slides and portamento, and make MIDI feel played rather than programmed.

lessons.title: Drawing Melodies
lessons.body:
The Piano Roll is where MIDI becomes music. Every note you can hear in a synthesiser or sampler is placed here — as a coloured rectangle whose horizontal position is its timing, whose vertical position is its pitch, and whose width is its duration. Understanding how to navigate and edit the Piano Roll quickly is the single skill that separates producers who finish ideas from producers who endlessly tweak.

## Navigating the Piano Roll

The Piano Roll opens when you double-click a pattern block in the Playlist, or right-click a Channel Rack channel → **Piano Roll** (or press **F7** with the channel selected).

**The keyboard strip** runs along the left edge — this is your pitch reference. Middle C (C5 in FL Studio's notation) is the standard reference pitch. Notes higher on the grid are higher in pitch; lower rows are lower.

**Zooming**: scroll the mouse wheel vertically to zoom the time axis in and out. Hold **Ctrl** and scroll to zoom the pitch axis. Hold **Alt** and scroll to zoom both simultaneously. Find a zoom level where you can see roughly 1–2 bars clearly and individual note rows are distinguishable.

**Scrolling**: hold the middle mouse button and drag to pan around the grid. Or use the scrollbars at the bottom and right edges.

## The Draw Tool

The pencil icon in the Piano Roll toolbar selects the **draw tool** (shortcut: **D**). This is the mode you will spend most of your time in:

- **Left-click** on an empty grid cell to place a note
- **Left-click and drag right** to place a note and set its length in one motion
- **Right-click** an existing note to delete it
- **Left-click and drag** an existing note to move it (pitch and/or timing)
- **Drag the right edge** of an existing note to resize it

The draw tool is modal — if you click on empty space, you place a note; if you click on an existing note, you move it. This is the source of most beginner Piano Roll frustration: accidentally moving notes when you meant to place new ones. Take a moment to aim carefully.

## Quantisation and the Snap Grid

The snap grid controls the smallest time unit notes can be placed at. The grid selector in the Piano Roll toolbar shows the current snap division — typically `1/16` (one sixteenth note).

For melodies: `1/16` is a good default. For slower, more sustained lines: `1/8` or `1/4`. For detailed humanisation or swing: `1/32` or even **none** (free placement).

Change the snap setting with the dropdown, or hold **Alt** while placing notes to temporarily override the snap and place freely.

## Writing a Melody That Moves

A melody is a sequence of pitches with a rhythm. The two are inseparable — the same pitches in different rhythmic shapes are different melodies. Here is a practical framework for drawing lines that feel intentional:

**Use stepwise motion as your base.** Moving up or down one semitone at a time (chromatic) or staying within a scale (diatonic steps) creates smooth, singable lines. Most strong melodies are predominantly stepwise.

**Leaps create emphasis.** Jumping a fifth, an octave, or a third draws the listener's attention to the destination note. Use leaps deliberately — at emotional peaks, on the strongest beat of the bar, or to surprise after a long stepwise run.

**Rests are notes.** Silence is melodic information. A melody that runs continuously without gaps sounds breathless and mechanical. Place deliberate rests — especially before a phrase's peak note — to create anticipation.

**Shape the phrase.** A melodic phrase typically rises toward a peak and then falls, or vice versa. Think of the contour of the line as a shape: does it arc upward? Plateau and fall? Spike suddenly? Draw the shape first in your mind, then place the notes.

**Repeat with variation.** The most memorable melodies repeat a short motif (2–4 notes) and then vary it slightly. Draw a 2-note cell, repeat it a bar later at a different pitch, then vary the rhythm on the third repetition. This gives the listener something to hold onto while keeping them interested.

## Practical Starting Point

If you are staring at a blank Piano Roll with no idea where to start:

1. Place a note at C5 on beat 1, lasting one beat
2. Place the same note a bar later, but move it up 2 semitones (to D5)
3. Place it a third time at E5, shorter — half a beat
4. Add a longer note at G5 to end the phrase

You now have a 4-note rising phrase with a rhythmic shape. That is a melody. Edit it, extend it, vary it. The blank page problem disappears the moment you have any starting material.
keyTakeaways: In the Piano Roll, horizontal position = timing, vertical position = pitch, note width = duration. Zoom with the scroll wheel, pan with middle-click drag, and use D to switch to the draw tool.
Left-click places a note; right-click deletes it; drag a note's right edge to resize it. The snap grid controls the smallest placeable division — 1/16 is the right default for most melodic work.

Strong melodies are mostly stepwise (scale steps), use leaps deliberately for emphasis, include intentional rests, and repeat a short motif with variation. Contour — the rise and fall of the line — is as important as the specific pitches.
lessons.tryThis: Open a Piano Roll for any synth channel. Set the snap to 1/16. Draw a four-bar melody using only these rules: start on C5, move no more than 3 semitones at a time (stepwise), include at least two rests of a full beat each, and end the phrase on C5 or G5. Don't worry if it sounds simple — simple is correct at this stage. Then listen back and ask: does it rise somewhere? Does it breathe? Does it feel like a phrase with a beginning and an end? Adjust one note at a time until the answer to all three is yes.

lessons.title: Chord Stacks That Sound Good
lessons.body:
You do not need music theory to build chords that work. You need to know one thing: **chords are built from intervals, and an interval is just a count of semitones between two notes.** Everything else follows from that.

This lesson gives you six chord shapes as semitone formulas, three progressions that work in almost any genre, and the voicing rules that keep chords from sounding muddy.

## Intervals as Semitone Counts

A semitone is the smallest step on a keyboard — one piano key, one fret on a guitar. Count from any root note upward:

| Semitones from root | Name | Sound |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | Major 2nd | Open, suspended |
| 3 | Minor 3rd | Dark, minor feel |
| 4 | Major 3rd | Bright, major feel |
| 5 | Perfect 4th | Strong, suspended |
| 7 | Perfect 5th | Neutral, powerful |
| 10 | Minor 7th | Smooth, jazzy |
| 11 | Major 7th | Dreamy, sophisticated |

Memoise these seven numbers. They are all you need.

## Six Essential Chord Shapes

All chords below are written as semitones above the root. To build any of them: place the root note in the Piano Roll, then place additional notes the specified number of semitones higher.

**Major triad** — `0 + 4 + 7`
The foundational bright chord. C major: C + E + G.

**Minor triad** — `0 + 3 + 7`
The foundational dark chord. C minor: C + Eb + G.

**Sus2** — `0 + 2 + 7`
Replaces the 3rd with a 2nd. Sounds open, modern, ambiguous — neither major nor minor. Extremely common in pop and electronic music.

**Sus4** — `0 + 5 + 7`
Replaces the 3rd with a 4th. Creates tension that resolves naturally to a major or minor chord. Useful before the "landing" chord in a progression.

**Minor 7** — `0 + 3 + 7 + 10`
A minor chord with a smooth top layer. Common in neo-soul, lo-fi, R&B. Sounds more sophisticated than a plain minor triad without being complicated.

**Major 7** — `0 + 4 + 7 + 11`
A major chord with a dreamy, floating quality. The 11-semitone note (the major 7th) creates the characteristic "cinema" or "luxury" sound heard in lo-fi hip-hop and sophisticated pop.

## Voicing: Where You Put the Notes Matters

The same chord sounds completely different depending on which octave range you place it in.

**The danger zone**: chords below C3 (especially with thirds and sevenths) produce muddy, indistinct sound because the harmonics of low notes overlap and clash. The lower you go, the simpler the chord needs to be.

**The sweet spot for full chords**: C3 to C5. Most chord voicings sound clear and balanced in this two-octave window.

**Spread for width**: instead of stacking all chord notes within one octave, move the top note up an additional octave. A C minor chord voiced as C3-Eb3-G3 sounds compressed; voiced as C3-Eb3-G4 it sounds open and wide.

**Remove the root for pads**: if you have a separate bass instrument handling the root note, remove the root from your chord voicing. A rootless voicing (Eb3-G3-Bb3 for a C minor 7, omitting the C) sounds airier and leaves space for the bass.

## Inversions: Moving the Bottom Note

An inversion puts a chord note other than the root at the bottom:

- **Root position**: C-E-G (root at bottom)
- **First inversion**: E-G-C (3rd at bottom — move the C up an octave)
- **Second inversion**: G-C-E (5th at bottom — move both C and E up)

Inversions keep the chord's harmonic identity while changing its bass note and its "gravity" — how much it wants to move. Second inversion chords feel unstable and want to resolve. Use them intentionally before a landing chord.

In practice: when moving between two chords, find the inversion that requires the least movement in the Piano Roll. If C major is C3-E3-G3 and you are moving to A minor, use A2-E3-A3 (first inversion A minor) rather than jumping everything up. Smooth voice leading — minimal movement between chords — is the difference between a progression that flows and one that lurches.

## Three Progressions That Work

Here are three chord progressions as semitone stacks from a root of C, ready to place directly in the Piano Roll. Each chord lasts one bar at 4/4.

**The emotional minor** (common in lo-fi, trap, emotional pop):
Bars 1–2: C minor (C3-Eb3-G3) → Bars 3–4: Ab major (Ab2-C3-Eb3)
Repeat, or extend with Bb major (Bb2-D3-F3) as bar 5–6.

**The floating sus2 loop** (common in electronic, pop):
Bar 1: C sus2 (C3-D3-G3) → Bar 2: G sus2 (G2-A2-D3) → Bar 3: A minor (A2-C3-E3) → Bar 4: F major (F2-A2-C3)

**The lo-fi loop** (common in jazz-influenced hip-hop):
Bar 1: C major 7 (C3-E3-G3-B3) → Bar 2: A minor 7 (A2-C3-E3-G3) → Repeat.

These are starting points, not rules. Change one chord. Substitute a sus2 for a minor. Add a 7th to everything. The formula gives you a working foundation to edit from.
keyTakeaways: Chords are built by stacking semitone intervals above a root. Memorise six shapes: major (0-4-7), minor (0-3-7), sus2 (0-2-7), sus4 (0-5-7), minor 7 (0-3-7-10), major 7 (0-4-7-11). You can build any of them from any root in the Piano Roll.

Keep full chord voicings between C3 and C5. Chords below C3 sound muddy — the lower you go, the simpler the chord must be. Spread the top note up an octave for width; remove the root if a separate bass instrument covers it.

Smooth voice leading — moving between chords with minimal note movement — makes progressions flow rather than lurch. Find the inversion of each chord that sits closest to the previous one rather than always playing root position.
lessons.tryThis: Open a Piano Roll for a pad or synth sound. Build the lo-fi loop: place a C major 7 chord (C3-E3-G3-B3) for bar 1 and an A minor 7 chord (A2-C3-E3-G3) for bar 2. Notice that three of the four notes stay the same or move by just one step — that is smooth voice leading in action. Now loop these two bars and listen. Add a bass note (just a single C2 for bar 1, A1 for bar 2) in a separate channel below. How much richer does the same chord voicing feel with a bass underneath? That two-element combination — simple rootless chord + single bass note — is the foundation of most lo-fi and neo-soul production.

lessons.title: Ghost Notes
lessons.body:
Ghost notes in the Piano Roll are semi-transparent previews of notes from other channels, displayed as a reference while you edit the current channel. They show you what other instruments are playing at the same time, so you can align pitches, lock rhythms, and make compositional decisions in context rather than in isolation.

Without ghost notes, writing a bass line means guessing where the kick drum falls and hoping the melody lands on compatible notes. With ghost notes, you see both simultaneously and can make deliberate choices.

## Enabling Ghost Notes

In the Piano Roll, look for the ghost note toggle in the toolbar — it looks like a small transparent note icon, or find it under **View → Ghost channels**. When enabled, FL Studio displays the notes of every other channel in the same pattern as faint, washed-out rectangles behind your active channel's notes.

You can also right-click the ghost note button to choose *which* channels appear as ghosts — useful when you have many channels and only want to see one or two as reference.

## Locking a Bass Line to the Kick

The most common use of ghost notes in drum-forward genres (hip-hop, trap, house, drum and bass): writing a bass line that locks rhythmically with the kick drum.

1. Open the Piano Roll for the bass channel
2. Enable ghost notes — you will see the kick drum's notes appear as faint blocks at whatever pitch the kick is mapped to
3. Place your bass notes so they align with or deliberately offset from the kick hits

The classic locked pattern: bass note lands exactly on the kick on beat 1, and also on any additional kick hits. The bass and kick share the same attack timing, creating a single fused low-end event rather than two competing elements.

The classic offset pattern: bass note lands one 16th note *after* each kick hit — the kick punches, then the bass follows. This creates a rolling, pushing feel common in house and certain hip-hop grooves.

With ghost notes, both approaches are deliberate. Without them, both are guesswork.

## Aligning Melody to Chords

When writing a melody over a chord progression, ghost notes show the chord notes from the pad or harmony channel. You can see exactly which notes the chord contains at each moment and make pitch choices that relate to them:

- Landing on a chord tone (one of the notes in the chord) at a strong beat creates resolution and stability
- Landing on a non-chord tone (a note *not* in the chord) on a strong beat creates tension that asks to resolve
- Passing through non-chord tones on weak beats or short note durations sounds smooth and melodic

With ghost notes visible, you can see the chord spelling and make these decisions visually — place the melody note on a chord tone for the downbeat, then let it step away briefly before returning.

## Aligning Counter-Melodies and Harmony

When adding a second melodic line (a counter-melody, a harmony, or a response phrase) to an existing one:

1. Open the Piano Roll for the second instrument
2. Enable ghost notes to see the first melody
3. Write the second line in relation to what you see

For a harmony: place notes a consistent interval above the ghost notes (3 or 4 semitones for a third harmony, 7 for a fifth). Because you can see the original melody, the harmony moves exactly with it without having to remember every note.

For a counter-melody: write a line that moves in the *opposite* direction to the ghost melody — when the main melody goes up, the counter-melody goes down. This creates independent motion that sounds like two distinct voices rather than one melody doubled.

## Practical Tip: Colour Code Your Channels

Ghost notes inherit the colour of their source channel. If all your channels are the same default colour, ghost notes are hard to distinguish at a glance. Colour-code your channels before you start composing — kick in red, bass in orange, chords in blue, lead in green — and the ghost note colours become an instant visual key.
keyTakeaways: Ghost notes display other channels' notes as faint references in the active Piano Roll. Enable them via View → Ghost channels or the toolbar toggle. Right-click the button to select which channels appear.

Use ghost notes to lock a bass line to the kick: place bass hits exactly on kick hits (fused attack) or one 16th note after (rolling feel). Both are deliberate choices only possible when you can see the kick pattern.
When writing melody over chords, ghost notes show the chord spelling. Land on chord tones for stability, non-chord tones for tension. The choice becomes visual rather than guesswork.
lessons.tryThis: Open the Piano Roll for a bass channel in a pattern that also has a kick drum. Enable ghost notes. You should see the kick's notes appear as faint markers. Now write a bass line: place bass notes directly on top of each kick hit. Loop the pattern and listen — the kick and bass should hit as one. Then try shifting every bass note one 16th note to the right of each kick hit. Loop again. Which feel suits your track more? That single timing decision, made visible by ghost notes, is one of the most important groove choices in bass-forward music.

lessons.title: Slides and Portamento
lessons.body:
A slide is a continuous pitch movement from one note to another — instead of jumping discretely from C to G, the pitch glides through all the frequencies in between. Portamento is the synthesiser setting that controls whether and how fast that slide happens. Together they are responsible for some of the most expressive sounds in electronic music: the 808 bass glide that defines trap, the mournful bend of a lead synth, the smooth legato movement of a string section.

## Slide Notes in the Piano Roll

FL Studio implements slides as special note events in the Piano Roll. A slide note tells the instrument: instead of retriggering the oscillator at this new pitch, slide the pitch continuously from wherever it currently is to this target pitch.

To add a slide note:
1. In the Piano Roll, right-click on an existing note
2. Choose **Note properties** (or press **Alt+P**)
3. Enable the **Slide** checkbox — the note turns a different colour (typically orange or a faded variant) to indicate slide behaviour

Alternatively: in some FL Studio versions, hold **Shift** while placing a note to create a slide note directly.

The slide note needs to immediately follow the previous note in the same channel for the slide to take effect. The duration of the slide note controls how long the glide takes — a short slide note = fast glide, a long slide note = slow glide.

## Portamento on Instruments

Slide notes only work when the instrument supports pitch slides. Most FL Studio synthesisers do, but the portamento (glide) setting on the instrument itself also affects behaviour.

In **3xOsc** and most FL Studio native synths: open the instrument, go to the **FUNC** tab, and find the **Portamento** knob. This sets the default glide time when notes slide. Higher values = slower, longer glides.

Important: portamento only applies to **monophonic** (single-note) playing. If two notes overlap — if the previous note is still held when the next one starts — the slide triggers. If there is a gap between notes, the pitch jumps instead of slides. This is why 808 bass slides require the note durations to overlap: the previous note must still be playing when the slide note begins.

## The 808 Bass Slide

Trap's signature sound — the 808 bass glide — is built entirely from this mechanism:

1. Load a long 808 sample (a sustained bass sub hit) in the Sampler or use a sine-wave synthesiser
2. In the Piano Roll, place a long note at C2 lasting through most of a bar
3. Place the next note (e.g. G1) so it *overlaps* with the end of the C2 note — the tail of C2 and the start of G1 share time
4. Make the G1 note a slide note
5. Set portamento to a medium value on the instrument

The result: C2 sustains, then glides down to G1 over the overlap duration. The pitch bends continuously rather than jumping. This is the entire mechanics behind every 808 slide in trap production.

## Slide Speed and Musical Timing

The speed of the slide is controlled by two things working together:
- **The overlap duration** — how long the previous note and the slide note coexist before the previous note ends
- **The portamento knob value** on the instrument

A slide that takes exactly one beat feels rhythmically musical — the listener hears a downward or upward sweep that completes on the next beat. A very fast slide (short overlap, low portamento) sounds like a pitch flick or grace note. A very slow slide (long overlap, high portamento) creates the impression of a theremin or bowed string.

## Portamento for Leads and Melodies

Portamento on a lead synth creates legato — smoothly connected notes without retriggering the oscillator's envelope on each pitch. This is how classic synthesiser solos achieve an organic, played-in-one-breath quality:

1. In the Piano Roll, draw the melody with notes that overlap slightly (each note ends just after the next one begins)
2. On the instrument, set a low portamento value — just enough to smooth the transitions without creating an obvious glide
3. The result sounds played and expressive rather than mechanically sequenced

For even more control: alternate between overlapping notes (which trigger the slide) and notes with a gap (which retrigger the envelope cleanly, giving a sharper attack for emphasis). This gives you dynamic control over which note transitions glide and which punch.
keyTakeaways: A slide note glides the pitch from the previous note to the new one rather than jumping. Right-click a note → Note properties → enable Slide. The slide note's duration controls how long the glide takes.

Portamento only triggers when notes overlap — the previous note must still be playing when the slide note starts. Gaps between notes cause a clean retrigger. This is why 808 bass slides require overlapping notes in the Piano Roll.

Low portamento on a lead with slightly overlapping notes creates legato — a smooth, connected, played-in-one-breath quality. Alternate overlapping and gapped notes to control which transitions glide and which punch.
lessons.tryThis: Build an 808-style bass slide. Load a low sine wave or 808 sample. In the Piano Roll, place a note at C2 lasting 3 beats. Place a second note at G1 starting half a beat before the C2 note ends (so they overlap by half a beat) and make it a slide note. Set portamento on the instrument to a medium value. Loop and listen — you should hear the pitch glide from C2 down to G1. Now adjust the overlap length: more overlap = slower glide, less overlap = faster. Find the slide speed that sounds right for your track's tempo. Then try the same technique going upward — from G1 to C2 — and hear how an upward slide changes the emotional character.

lessons.title: Humanising with Velocity and Timing
lessons.body:
When every note is the same velocity and lands exactly on the grid, MIDI sounds like a spreadsheet. The notes are technically correct but they lack the micro-variations that a human performer produces involuntarily — the slight push or pull of timing, the natural accent on strong beats, the decay of energy toward the end of a phrase. Humanisation is the practice of introducing those variations deliberately.

The goal is not to make MIDI sound like a live recording. The goal is to make it feel intentional and alive rather than mechanical and rigid. The two are different — a programmed beat at 90 BPM can feel deeply human without pretending to be a drummer in a room.

## Velocity: The Primary Expressive Control

Velocity controls how hard a note is struck — in practice, how loud it is, and sometimes how bright or soft the timbre is (on velocity-sensitive instruments). In the Piano Roll, each note has a velocity value from 1 to 127.

The velocity editor appears at the bottom of the Piano Roll as a row of vertical bars — one per note. Taller bars = higher velocity. Click and drag any bar to adjust its value.

**The fundamental rule of musical velocity**: strong beats should be louder than weak beats. In 4/4 time:
- Beat 1 (the downbeat): highest velocity — the natural accent
- Beat 3: secondary accent — slightly softer than beat 1
- Beats 2 and 4: lighter — these are the backbeats in most genres
- Subdivisions (the "and"s and "e"s): softest — these are fills and passing notes

Applying this to a hi-hat pattern: beats 1 and 3 at velocity 100, beats 2 and 4 at velocity 80, all 16th-note in-between hits at velocity 50–65. The same 16-step pattern sounds completely different — it breathes and grooves instead of mechanically ticking.

## Velocity for Melodic Expression

For melody and chords, velocity communicates the shape and emphasis of the phrase:

- **Crescendo**: gradually increasing velocity across a phrase makes it feel like it is building toward something
- **Accent the peak note**: the highest pitch in a melodic phrase is usually the emotional peak — give it the highest velocity in the phrase
- **Ghost the passing notes**: fast notes that connect two main notes (passing tones) should be quieter — they are decoration, not the statement
- **Release naturally**: velocity at the end of a phrase should taper down, as if the performer is relaxing their intensity

## Timing: Pushing and Pulling the Grid

Timing variation — notes landing slightly before or after the grid — is what separates a groove from a sequence. Specifically:

**Pushing ahead** (notes slightly before the beat): creates urgency, tension, excitement. A snare that lands 10ms early sounds aggressive. A melody note that anticipates the chord creates a leaning-forward feel.

**Pulling back** (notes slightly after the beat): creates weight, laid-back feel, relaxation. A kick that lands 10ms late feels heavier. A vocal that sits behind the beat has that characteristic hip-hop and R&B ease.

To nudge notes in FL Studio: select a note (or multiple notes) and use **Shift+Left/Right arrow** to move them by the current snap resolution, or **Alt+Left/Right** to nudge by one tick (the smallest time unit). For very fine nudges, right-click a note → **Note properties** and adjust the **Time** value directly.

## FL Studio's Humanize Function

For a quick starting point, FL Studio includes a built-in randomisation tool:

**Tools → Humanize** (or **Ctrl+U** in the Piano Roll)

The Humanize dialog lets you add random variation to:
- **Velocity**: randomly offsets each note's velocity within a range you set
- **Timing**: randomly offsets each note's position within a range
- **Note length**: randomly varies each note's duration

Start with small values — velocity variation of ±10–15, timing variation of 5–10 ticks. Large values sound sloppy rather than human. Apply humanise, listen, undo if it pushes too far, and reapply with smaller values.

Humanise is most effective on repeated patterns: a 16-bar hi-hat loop where every hit is identical benefits greatly from a small velocity pass. A carefully crafted melody that already has intentional velocity shaping does not need it.

## The LFO Tool on Velocity

For systematic velocity shaping rather than randomisation, FL Studio's **LFO tool** can write velocity values as a curve across a selection of notes:

1. Select all notes in a bar (Ctrl+A)
2. Go to **Tools → LFO tool**
3. Set the shape (sine gives a gentle wave, triangle gives a more mechanical pulse)
4. Set the speed to match the phrase length — one LFO cycle per bar, for example
5. Set the target to **Velocity** and apply

The result: velocity rises and falls across the selected notes in a smooth wave pattern, creating an automatic crescendo-and-decay that repeats every bar. This is faster than drawing every velocity bar manually and sounds more intentional than randomisation.

## Combining Both: The Professional Approach

Most production involves layering these techniques:

1. Draw the MIDI with default velocity
2. Apply a broad velocity shape manually — accent the strong beats, taper the ends of phrases
3. Apply a small Humanize pass (±8 velocity, 3–5 tick timing variation) to add micro-variation on top of the shaped values
4. Go back and manually adjust any note whose velocity or timing feels wrong after the randomisation

This combination gives you intentional musical shape with natural-feeling imperfection on top — the two qualities that make programmed MIDI convincing.
keyTakeaways: Velocity should follow musical emphasis: strongest on the downbeat (beat 1), lighter on beats 2 and 4, softest on 16th-note subdivisions. This one principle applied to any repeating pattern immediately adds groove.

Timing variation — pushing notes ahead of the beat for urgency, pulling them back for weight — is what creates groove feel. Use Shift+arrow or Alt+arrow to nudge selected notes by the snap increment or by one tick.

Humanize (Ctrl+U) adds random velocity and timing variation automatically. Use small values (±10–15 velocity, 5–10 ticks) as a finishing pass on already-shaped patterns. Large Humanize values sound sloppy, not human.
lessons.tryThis: Take any 2-bar MIDI pattern you have already written — a hi-hat, a melody, a chord stab. Open the velocity editor at the bottom of the Piano Roll. Manually draw the velocity bars so that every note landing on beat 1 or beat 3 is the tallest, beat 2 and 4 notes are medium, and any 16th-note fills are short. Listen to the difference before and after. Then apply Humanize (Ctrl+U) with a small velocity variation of ±12 and a timing variation of 5 ticks. Listen again. Does it feel more natural? If one note now sounds obviously wrong (randomly too quiet or too early), click Undo, adjust that bar manually first, then re-apply Humanize. That iterative process — shape, randomise, fix outliers — is the standard professional workflow.
